title: Fasting and Lent - Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Fasting and Lent - Part 2 - Matthew 9_14-16 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review and Resources
subpoints: Brief overview of last week: fasting is not just not eating, but hungering for God
Recommended resources: John Piper's Hunger for God, David Mathis's Habits of Grace, Don Whitney's Spiritual Disciplines for the Christian Life
The foundational reason for fasting: we fast because we hunger for God
Fasting is a voluntary discipline, not a church-imposed requirement

outline.title: Matthew 9:14-17: Fasting and the Presence of the Bridegroom
subpoints: John's disciples and the Pharisees fasted regularly; Jesus' disciples did not
Jesus answers: you don't mourn while the bridegroom is present
When the bridegroom is taken away, then they will fast
New cloth on old garments and new wine in old wineskins illustrate new-covenant continuity

outline.title: What It Means to Fast While the Bridegroom Is Away
subpoints: The 'taking away' of the bridegroom refers not to the three days in the tomb but to the period between Christ's ascension and return
We live in the 'already and not yet': Christ is spiritually present but not yet physically with us
New covenant fasting is not mourning over unforgiven sin but longing for the physical return of Christ
Christian fasting is still fasting - a new and better discipline, but continuous with the Old Testament pattern

outline.title: Isaiah 58: True Fasting Defined by God
subpoints: The prophet confronts Israel's self-righteous fasting: they fast yet exploit their workers and fight among themselves
God rejects fasting that is mere outward performance divorced from a transformed heart
True fasting, as God defines it, is loosing bonds of wickedness, freeing the oppressed, feeding the hungry, and clothing the naked
Fasting goes beyond not eating - it is the total submission of life to the reign of King Jesus, expressed in service to others

outline.title: Summary and Practical Conclusions
subpoints: Fasting is part of the Christian life as a voluntary discipline, not a mandatory one
Fasting is a private exercise not to be publicized
Fasting points to the fact that this world is not all there is; we crave the coming of Christ
Fasting can extend beyond food to any good thing we give up to hunger for God: social media, entertainment, habits

summary:
In this second session of the Fasting and Lent series, Pastor Tito Lyro examines two key passages that deepen the church's understanding of what biblical fasting is. He opens with a brief review of the prior week and recommends three resources - John Piper's Hunger for God, David Mathis's Habits of Grace, and Don Whitney's Spiritual Disciplines for the Christian Life - capturing the core idea that we fast because we hunger for God, not merely to achieve religious merit.

The first passage is Matthew 9:14-17, where the disciples of John ask why Jesus' disciples do not fast. Jesus' answer centers on the presence of the bridegroom: fasting is inappropriate while the bridegroom is present, but it becomes fitting when he is taken away. Lyro argues that this refers not to the three days of Christ's burial but to the entire period between the Ascension and the Second Coming. We fast now - in the 'not yet' of Christ's physical absence - while resting in the assurance of the 'already': our sins are forgiven and our King reigns. The parables of the cloth and wineskins affirm that new covenant fasting is genuinely continuous with Old Testament fasting, not abolished but renewed and deepened.

The second major passage is Isaiah 58:1-12, where God confronts His people's hollow religious fasting. They fasted outwardly while exploiting their employees and fighting among themselves - a picture of Sunday-only Christianity. God rejects this kind of performative religion and declares that true fasting means loosing bonds of wickedness, feeding the hungry, and clothing the naked. Fasting is thus not merely abstaining from food; it is the total submission of life to the reign of Christ, of which abstaining from food is only a token.

The session closes with practical conclusions: fasting is a voluntary discipline that is not publicized, points beyond this world to the coming of Christ, and may encompass abstaining from any good thing (not just food) for the sake of a greater hunger for God.
transcript:
## Review and Recommended Resources

We're continuing our series on fasting. We're now in that period some Christians call Lent, which is often marked by fasting or by abstaining from some luxury or comfort. Lent is not a biblical festival — it's not a biblical feast. And at this point, I think Christians shouldn't feel obligated to practice Lent. It's not a biblical institution. But the Bible does talk about fasting, and that gives us a good opportunity to consider the subject.

Last week we looked at Matthew 6:16-18, where Jesus teaches about fasting. Tonight we'll look at two other passages, Lord willing, that will throw more light on the subject — or perhaps just muddy the waters further. We'll see. Lord willing, we'll get to both of them tonight.

Three resources I want to mention. The first is a book by John Piper called *A Hunger for God*. You can get it for free as a PDF from desiringgod.org. The second is *Habits of Grace* by David Mathis — every family was given a copy this week, and it has a chapter on fasting. The third is *Spiritual Disciplines for the Christian Life* by Don Whitney, which is a classic and also addresses fasting. These three resources would be good for you to read as you think about this subject.

### Quick Review

A quick overview of what we've covered so far. What is fasting? That question might seem easy to answer — not eating. That's what I had to do Saturday when I went to get my blood drawn: they wanted to measure everything without any food on board. But as we're going to see, there's more to fasting than just not eating.

What is the *why* of fasting? There are some important secondary reasons for fasting, but the foundational reason is captured well in Piper's title for his book: we fast because we hunger for God. That's the basic idea. Isn't that how we are described in Matthew 5:6? "Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be filled." And isn't that what David says in Psalm 63:1:

> *"O God, You are my God; early will I seek You; my soul thirsts for You; my flesh longs for You in a dry and thirsty land where there is no water."* (Psalm 63:1)

That's what fasting is showing — that we hunger and thirst after God. And remember, we're not getting rid of something bad when we fast. Piper says that fasting is not the forfeit of the evil but of the good. We're giving up something we like. We deprive ourselves of the good because we desire the best. That's why we fast. And the how: with humility, unto the Lord, not for the praise of men. That summarizes last week.

So this evening we're going to add two passages: Matthew 9 and Isaiah 58.

## Matthew 9:14-17: Fasting and the Presence of the Bridegroom

Let's start with Matthew chapter 9. We'll read verses 14 through 17. Jesus says:

> *"Then the disciples of John came to Him, saying, 'Why do we and the Pharisees fast often, but Your disciples do not fast?' And Jesus said to them, 'Can the friends of the bridegroom mourn as long as the bridegroom is with them? But the days will come when the bridegroom will be taken away from them, and then they will fast. No one puts a piece of unshrunk cloth on an old garment; for the patch pulls away from the garment, and the tear is made worse. Nor do they put new wine into old wineskins, or else the wineskins break, the wine is spilled, and the wineskins are ruined. But they put new wine into new wineskins, and both are preserved.'"* (Matthew 9:14-17)

The question brought to Jesus tells us that his disciples — and presumably Jesus himself — did not follow a schedule for religious fasting the way the Pharisees did, or the way John's disciples did. The questioners are surprised. But Jesus' disciples were not following the dictated schedule for fasting that the Pharisees and John's disciples followed.

Jesus says that while he is with them, they do not have to fast — instead they must rejoice. This implies that fasting has something to do with longing for the presence of Jesus. If you are with me, you do not fast. If you are without me, then you fast. So there is an appropriate time for his disciples to fast, and that time is when he is no longer with them.

This is, by the way, the only other passage in the Bible where Jesus himself teaches about fasting — Matthew 6 and here in Matthew 9.

### When Is the Bridegroom Taken Away?

So when is the time that Jesus will not be with them? That is when they are supposed to fast.

For a while, I thought this was a reference to the three days Jesus was in the tomb. I thought that because one of the first things Jesus did when he met with the disciples after the resurrection was to feed them. All the accounts of the upper room and then the Sea of Galilee involve them eating. The one who had been taken away by wicked hands, as Acts 2 says, had come back to them. And he soon sent the other Comforter who, just like him, would stay with them forever. And the promise he made in the Great Commission — "I am with you always" (Matthew 28:20) — is there. So if that's the right interpretation, there is no need for Christians to fast because Christ is always present with them.

The only problem with that interpretation is that it doesn't make sense of verses 16 and 17 — the cloth and the wineskins. How does that fit? That kept bothering me. I liked the idea that we didn't have to fast — that would be great! But it doesn't really work for the whole passage.

### New Covenant Continuity

So what do we do here? I think these two verses point to at least two things about fasting and about the relationship of the new covenant to the old.

In Christ, some things have changed from the old to the new covenant. Christ fulfilled some things that the people of God under the old covenant did, and we no longer do them. We don't fast on the Day of Atonement wondering if our sins will be forgiven, because we know that our high priest stands for us day and night pleading on our behalf (1 John 2). We don't fast in anticipation of the Messiah's arrival because he has already come.

But in Christ, some things remain the same. What Christ brings is new and better — but it is still *cloth*. The parable doesn't say you sew with a different material; it's still cloth, just new cloth. Christ brings new and better *wine* — but still wine. So there is continuity between what the saints did before the cross and what we do today. The fast that Jesus' disciples practice is still a fast — a new and better one, a new covenant fast. Not wondering whether our sins will be forgiven, but knowing that our sins are forgiven and desiring more of the One who forgave them.

### The Already and Not Yet

It also points to the fact that we live between times — the already and the not yet. Our Redeemer has already come and established his kingdom. We are living in his kingdom right now and he is with us. But as a mediated kingdom, the King is not here physically. So we long for the day our King will return — a return guaranteed by the cross. And so we fast. But we fast in the full assurance that our King is with us, even though not yet physically. That's the already: the King is with us. That's the not yet: not physically, not yet.

We fast while the bridegroom is away — not referring to the three days between the cross and the resurrection, but to the days between his ascension and his return. We long for that day.

## Isaiah 58: True Fasting Defined by God

The second passage is Isaiah 58. Isaiah 58:1-12 is much like Matthew 6:16-18 that we saw last week — a correction of the people's practice of fasting. The prophet is to confront God's people with their self-righteousness. Look at verses 1 through 3:

> *"Cry aloud, spare not; lift up your voice like a trumpet; tell My people their transgression, and the house of Jacob their sins. Yet they seek Me daily, and delight to know My ways, as a nation that did righteousness, and did not forsake the ordinance of their God. They ask of Me the ordinances of justice; they take delight in approaching God. 'Why have we fasted,' they say, 'and You have not seen? Why have we afflicted our souls, and You take no notice?'"* (Isaiah 58:1-3)

God is not saying these things are true of them. He's saying they are *acting as if* they were true. They were claiming to be the greatest, most obedient people ever — but God is about to tell the whole world how sinful they really are, how they have not practiced what is right. And yet they say, look how obedient we are, God must be about to bless us because we're so good.

### Self-Righteous Fasting

Even their fasting was self-righteous. They were essentially saying, "Here, God — I'll do this fast for you so that you become obligated to do something for me." That's why they're asking in verse 3: "We did our part. We fasted. How come you haven't done anything for us?"

And while fasting supposedly unto the Lord, their lives were completely ruled by their wicked hearts. Look at the second half of verse 3 through verse 5:

> *"In fact, in the day of your fast you find pleasure, and exploit all your laborers. Indeed you fast for strife and debate, and to strike with the fist of wickedness. You will not fast as you do this day, to make your voice heard on high. Is it a fast that I have chosen, a day for a man to afflict his soul? Is it to bow down his head like a bulrush, and to spread out sackcloth and ashes? Would you call this a fast, and an acceptable day to the Lord?"* (Isaiah 58:3b-5)

God says: what you're doing is not fasting. At least not the kind of fast acceptable to me. They're exploiting their employees while fasting. They're saying, "What we're doing for the Lord is a great thing" — and then they're being cruel to their servants. They're fighting with each other while fasting — maybe even a little hangry. They were doing a fast that was not to please the Lord, but to look good as religious people.

They are much like the person who comes to church on Sunday and sings loudly, maybe even closes their eyes to show how into the song they are, nods in agreement with the sermon — and then goes home to live wickedly as if nothing had happened. That is exactly what's going on here. If you are that person, God says to you: this will not do. You are deceiving yourself if you think your Sunday Christianity is anything to be proud of. God is not interested in the appearance of fasting. God is not interested in the appearance of being religious. He is interested in true hearts that are worshiping him from the heart.

### God's Definition of True Fasting

This passage is really important to our study because here God defines fasting not as abstention from food but as *loving him by serving others*. Look at verses 6 and 7:

> *"Is this not the fast that I have chosen: to loose the bonds of wickedness, to undo the heavy burdens, to let the oppressed go free, and that you break every yoke? Is it not to share your bread with the hungry, and that you bring to your house the poor who are cast out; when you see the naked, that you cover him, and not hide yourself from your own flesh?"* (Isaiah 58:6-7)

Fasting goes beyond just not eating. When you are not eating as a service to the Lord, you are serving others. You are loving God by loving and serving others. God says that true fasting is selfless service to others because of God's reign in your heart. Just putting on a show for other people to see is deceiving yourself — nobody else is being deceived. God is certainly not going to be deceived. He knows your heart, and it's the heart he's looking for.

In verse 9 he says: then you shall call, and the Lord will answer; you shall cry, and he will say, "Here I am" — when you serve him from the heart. If you extend your soul to the hungry and satisfy the afflicted soul, then your light shall dawn in the darkness... the Lord will guide you continually and satisfy your soul in drought and strengthen your bones. You shall be like a watered garden, and like a spring of water whose waters do not fail.

All these great things come from a heart that is truly worshiping the Lord. That's why it is acceptable to him. Jesus will be the satisfaction of their souls.

## What We Have Learned

So what have we learned so far?

Fasting is part of the Christian life as a voluntary discipline — not something mandatory like the Lord's Day. We are all committed to gathering together on the Lord's Day; if you don't do that, you're sinning. But fasting is something we enter voluntarily. We decide when we're going to do it. It's not going to be a proclamation from the pulpit or an imposition from the church.

We've also learned that fasting is a private exercise that you shouldn't publicize. You can't go around saying, "I'm fasting today, I'm so hungry, look how godly I am."

We've also learned that fasting points to the fact that this life and this world are not all there is. We're looking for the world to come. We're craving, hungry for the coming of the Lord Jesus Christ, longing for the day when we will be with our King and see him face to face. When you give up food, you meditate on that greater food.

And above all, we've learned that fasting goes beyond not eating. Fasting is the total submission to Jesus Christ, of which the occasion of abstaining from food is a token. Fasting is really about the rule of King Jesus in your life — about bringing everything, by God's grace, captive to Christ Jesus.

I hope that is true of all of us here today. And if it's not, now is the time to yield yourself to Jesus Christ. If Christ is not ruling sovereignly in your heart, if you have not given yourself to him, now is the time. You don't know if you have another second in your life. This fast is just a representation — a token — of the sovereign rule of Christ in your heart.

## What Is Christian Fasting?

The question remains: what is a Christian fast, and how do we practice it? Because we don't want to be just hearers of the word — we want to be doers. The Great Commission says Jesus' disciples are to observe all that Christ has commanded, not just to know it but to observe it.

Generally speaking, fasting is abstaining from food with the purpose of seeking the Lord. That's really what it is.

Now let me emphasize: food is good. We are to rejoice in it. Ecclesiastes says every man should eat and drink and enjoy the good of all his labor — it is a gift of God. It's important that we understand that food is good, and that any prohibition against eating for religious reasons is unbiblical. Paul is very clear about this in 1 Timothy 4, where he says that the Spirit expressly says that in latter times, some will depart from the faith, giving heed to deceiving spirits and doctrines of demons, forbidding to marry and commanding to abstain from foods which God created to be received with thanksgiving. Departing from the faith includes making food restrictions a religious requirement.

So we don't abstain from food because food is bad. We abstain from food *because food is good*. We recognize it as a good gift from God and fast to signify that, though we appreciate the gift, we are more interested in the Giver. I want to emphasize this because not eating can become an idol in itself, and food can be seen as the enemy — as in anorexia. That's not fasting. That's not biblical fasting. That's a sinful attitude toward your own body, toward life in general, toward other people.

### Fasting Can Be More Than Food

Fasting can also be more than abstaining from food. For some people, giving up food is not a big deal. But giving up social media would be close to destroying their world. I was listening to a podcast today about a book for women, and it mentioned that teenage girls today spend on average nine hours a day on social media — not for school, just nine hours daily. For some people, that would be a more meaningful fast than giving up food.

For others, abstaining from smoking or having a beer at the end of the day might be a more appropriate fast than abstaining from food. For others yet, abstaining from video games or TV. For some, abstaining from being constantly connected to other people — giving up the phone, giving up the texts, taking some time of solitude — might be more precious to give up than food.

So the possibilities are many. Though generally fasting has to do with food, the principle is: we're giving up something good because we're hungry for something better.

The Scriptures also indicate that when we fast, we are abstaining from something we enjoy — something that could potentially become an idol — not from things we don't enjoy. Nick and I committed to giving up kale for a season. That's not really fasting because we believe kale is a result of the Fall. That's just not a big deal. When we fast, we're saying: Lord, I love this thing, but I love you more.

So: giving up something good and righteous and godly for something even better — more of God himself. Lord willing, we'll be able to finish this up next Wednesday when we wrap up what Christian fasting is, review why we fast, and look at how we fast.
